title: npm Scripts and npx
description: Automate tasks with npm scripts and run packages with npx.

slides.title: npm Scripts – Your Project's Swiss Army Knife 🔧
slides.content:
# npm Scripts

The `scripts` field in `package.json` lets you define custom commands. They are handy for common tasks like starting a server, running tests, or building assets.

Example:
```json
"scripts": {
  "start": "node index.js",
  "dev": "nodemon index.js",
  "test": "jest",
  "build": "webpack --mode production"
}
```

Run them with:
```bash
npm run dev
npm test  # npm test is a special alias for npm run test
npm start # another alias
```

Scripts can access locally installed packages (like `nodemon`) because npm adds `./node_modules/.bin` to the PATH temporarily.

slides.title: Custom Script Example
slides.content:
Add this to package.json:
```json
"scripts": {
  "greet": "echo \"Hello from npm!\" && node -v"
}
```
Run with `npm run greet`.

slides.title: npx – Run Packages Without Installing 🏃
slides.content:
## npx

`npx` is a tool that comes with npm (since npm 5.2+). It lets you run a package without globally installing it.

Example:
```bash
npx cowsay Hello
```
This downloads `cowsay` temporarily (if not already installed) and runs it.

Use cases:
- Run one-off commands (e.g., `npx create-react-app my-app`).
- Execute a package that you don't want to install globally.
- Run a specific version of a package.

`npx` also checks if the package is in your local `node_modules` and runs it from there.

quizData.question: How do you run a script named 'build' defined in package.json?
quizData.explanation: Custom scripts must be run with `npm run <script>`. Only `start` and `test` are special aliases.
